segments.title: Why HMS Dreadnought marks a new battleship era
segments.summary: Dreadnought became the practical dividing line between earlier battleships and a new class of capital ship.
segments.body: The video begins by explaining why HMS Dreadnought is more than a famous individual ship: she gave her name to a whole era of battleship design. Her combination of features also shaped the later battlecruiser and fast-battleship lineage, making her a useful starting point for understanding twentieth-century capital ships.

evidence.note: The lecture treats Dreadnought as the line between pre-dreadnought and dreadnought battleships and traces a design lineage toward battlecruisers and fast battleships.

segments.title: An all-big-gun ship built for salvo fire
segments.summary: The all-big-gun battery mattered because a larger salvo could support accurate long-range engagement.
segments.body: Earlier pre-dreadnoughts commonly carried only a few heavy guns, useful for occasional long-range shots or close-range blows but poorly suited to sustained, accurate salvo fire. Dreadnought's heavier uniform main battery gave a commander enough shells in flight to observe and correct fire at ranges where mixed batteries were much harder to use effectively.

evidence.note: The speaker contrasts four-gun pre-dreadnought main batteries with Dreadnought's ability to deliver a larger long-range salvo.

segments.title: Wing turrets and Fisher's forward-fire priority
segments.summary: Dreadnought's wing turrets reflected a desire to bring six heavy guns to bear ahead, while hinting at other possible layouts.
segments.body: The design used wing turrets to improve the forward arc of fire, a choice rooted in contemporary pre-dreadnought practice. The discussion also shows how a different turret arrangement or a longer, narrower hull might have changed gun layout and speed, illustrating that even a landmark ship was a set of trade-offs rather than an inevitable final form.

evidence.note: The lecture discusses the possible use of superfiring or triple turrets, then explains the actual wing-turret arrangement and its six-gun forward fire.

segments.title: Speed plus range changes the engagement
segments.summary: At 21 knots, Dreadnought could choose distance and bring a long-range salvo that individual pre-dreadnoughts could not match.
segments.body: The key advantage was not simply a larger battery in isolation. Greater speed let Dreadnought shape an engagement, while her long-range firepower imposed a problem that older ships could answer only with favorable circumstances, local knowledge, or numerical concentration.

evidence.note: The speaker links 21-knot speed to Dreadnought's ability to engage at a range and with a volley older ships could not match.

segments.title: The ideas and people behind Dreadnought
segments.summary: Fisher drew on Lord Nelson components, long-range gunnery advocates, the Tsushima report, and turbine technology to make the ship possible.
segments.body: Dreadnought was not conceived in a vacuum: the preceding Lord Nelson class contributed gun and turret designs, while Pakenham's observations of Tsushima helped strengthen the case for change. The video also highlights Percy Scott's long-range gunnery advocacy and Charles Parsons's turbines, emphasizing that propulsion and fire control were as important as the calibre of the guns.

evidence.note: The lecture identifies Lord Nelson, Pakenham, Cuniberti, Percy Scott, and Parsons as contributors to the design's intellectual and technical background.

segments.title: Why steam turbines were central to Dreadnought
segments.summary: Turbines supplied sustained high-speed performance, making them as defining a feature as the all-big-gun battery.
segments.body: The video argues against reducing Dreadnought to an armament story alone. Turbine propulsion delivered the speed and endurance at speed that made her heavy guns operationally more useful, so the revolutionary effect came from the package of hull, machinery, armament, and fire control together.

evidence.note: The speaker calls turbines crucial to Dreadnought's differentiation because they supported prolonged high-speed operation.

segments.title: The practical machinery, armour, and secondary weapons
segments.summary: Dreadnought combined turbines, ten 12-inch guns, anti-destroyer weapons, torpedo tubes, and substantial armour in a single imposing hull.
segments.body: This technical overview puts the ship's famous main battery in its wider design context: 18,410 tons, turbines driving four shafts, a 21-knot speed, and a crew that grew from about 700 to 810. The ship still carried 12-pounders for protection against destroyers and torpedo tubes alongside its heavy armour, showing that an all-big-gun battleship did not abandon close defence or survivability.

evidence.note: The lecture gives displacement, machinery, speed, complement, gun, torpedo, and armour details, including the role of the 12-pounder secondary battery.

segments.title: Dreadnought as a bundle of combat capabilities
segments.summary: The term dreadnought is most useful when it describes a heavily armoured, turbine-powered ship able to strike at long range.
segments.body: Historical labels are only rough guides, but the video uses Dreadnought to identify a distinct combat system rather than a simple chronological category. Heavy armour, turbine power, long-range heavy guns, and a layout that could employ them together explain why merely building an all-big-gun ship was not the whole achievement.

evidence.note: The speaker defines the dreadnought concept through turbine power, armour, long-range punch, and the ability to use the combined capability.

segments.title: A deterrent meant to reset naval competition
segments.summary: Fisher hoped Dreadnought's superiority would discourage competitors, but it instead became a model for their own new battleships.
segments.body: The video presents Dreadnought as a deterrent gamble: an overwhelming lead might have persuaded other powers to stop competing and left Britain free to dictate its own pace. Instead, foreign navies began ordering dreadnoughts of their own, turning a British advantage into the benchmark for an accelerating arms race.

evidence.note: The lecture explains Fisher's hoped-for deterrent effect and the subsequent foreign orders for dreadnought-type battleships.

segments.title: Weight of fire forces every navy to catch up
segments.summary: Dreadnought restored a powerful long-range volume of fire and made catching up an urgent problem for other navies.
segments.body: The closing argument links Dreadnought to a broader change in naval combat. As ships had carried fewer but more powerful guns, range and rate of effective fire had not risen in step; a large battery of heavy guns made it possible to deliver both at distance and compelled rivals to seek an answer.

evidence.note: The speaker identifies weight of fire at range as the decisive change and describes other navies as having to catch up.
